body:
### Problem

Summary
Provide an opt‑in compatibility layer with scoped modes—“legacy” and “modern”—that can be selected globally, per‑buffer, per‑command, or per‑block, to allow legacy workflows to continue while enabling corrected/modernized semantics for new workflows. This enables progressive migration and safer experimentation without breaking existing plugins/scripts.

Motivation

    Many users and plugins rely on legacy Vim semantics (register behaviors, autocommand ordering, synchronous assumptions) that are error-prone but cannot be changed without breakage.
    Neovim’s modern APIs (async I/O, Lua, extmarks, LSP) enable corrected behaviors; offering a scoped compatibility mechanism allows:
        Running legacy code unchanged (compatibility).
        Opting into corrected behavior for specific commands, plugins, buffers, or projects.
        Incremental migration and safer plugin development.
    Scoped switching (per‑command / per‑block) is useful for transactional operations that need atomic, modern semantics without globally changing environment.

Design overview

    Mode scopes
        Global: default for the session (vim.g.compat_mode).
        Buffer/window/session: buffer-local or window-local override (b:compat_mode / w:compat_mode).
        Per-command: transient mode applied only for a single command invocation.
        Per-block/transaction: an explicit begin/end or a coroutine/context API that runs a block of Lua/Ex commands under a chosen mode.

    Core behaviors covered (initial subset)
        Register semantics (unnamed/clipboard/numbered registers, atomic yank/delete semantics).
        Autocommand/event ordering and sync/async boundaries.
        Clipboard interaction (OS provider semantics).
        Delete/yank clobber rules to avoid accidental register overwrites.
        Optional persistence for selected registers.

    API examples (Lua + Ex)
        Lua context: local compat = require('compat') compat.run_with_mode('modern', function() vim.cmd('normal! d') -- other operations run under modern semantics end)
        Ex command (per-command): :CompatExec modern %delete
        Block markers: :CompatBegin modern :%delete :CompatEnd
        Buffer override: :let b:compat_mode = 'modern'

    Implementation approach
        Start as a userland Lua plugin that intercepts a focused set of behaviors (register ops, yank/delete, autocommand dispatch) to prove the model.
        Provide lightweight, atomic flags and context managers to switch behavior with minimal overhead.
        Expand supported semantics incrementally with comprehensive tests.
        Offer detection/opt-in metadata for plugins that rely on legacy semantics (so hybrid mode can apply shims only where needed).

    Safety, atomicity, async concerns
        Mode switches must be atomic for the duration of commands/blocks; async jobs spawned within a modern block should capture the mode or default to a configured fallback.
        Provide warnings/logging for mode-sensitive plugins and recommend explicit plugin metadata to declare compatibility expectations.
        Keep the legacy shim lightweight to avoid performance penalties for the default mode.

    Migration & tooling
        Offer deprecation warnings when plugins/scripts rely on legacy semantics that have modern equivalents.
        Provide migration helper functions and test harnesses to run suites in both modes.

Requested next steps

    Initial feedback from core maintainers on scope and whether to start as a plugin-first effort.
    If accepted, guidance on API shape and contribution process.
    Volunteers or reviewers who can advise on async boundary semantics and tests.

Appendix: minimal pseudo-code (illustrative)
-- compat.lua
local M = {}
function M.run_with_mode(mode, fn)
local old = vim.g.compat_mode
vim.g.compat_mode = mode
local ok, res = pcall(fn)
vim.g.compat_mode = old
if not ok then error(res) end
return res
end
return M

### Expected behavior

Provide an opt‑in compatibility layer with scoped modes—“legacy” and “modern”—that can be selected globally, per‑buffer, per‑command, or per‑block, to allow legacy workflows to continue while enabling corrected/modernized semantics for new workflows. This enables progressive migration and safer experimentation without breaking existing plugins/scripts.

comment_data.body: Thank you for taking a look — I’m not a developer and don’t have a prototype to attach; this is a conceptual proposal. I’m open to feedback on scope, API shape, and a plugin‑first implementation path, and I’ll update the issue text based on maintainers’ guidance.

Language support (Vimscript + Lua) only changes how code is written; it does not change runtime semantics, option defaults, evaluation/coercion rules, autocommand/event ordering, or error propagation. Those semantic differences are the real compatibility surface that breaks plugins when global defaults change.

Why scoped compatibility is necessary:

    Preserve legacy behavior where required while keeping modern defaults elsewhere (per-command, per-plugin, per-buffer, per-filetype).
    Avoid broad regressions caused by a single global toggle.
    Enable targeted diagnostics and gradual migration (run a plugin/file under legacy mode to reproduce and fix issues without affecting the whole editor).

Concrete items to include in the design:

    Scopes: per-command, per-buffer, per-plugin, per-filetype, plus a global default.
    Toggleable rule-sets: option defaults, expression-evaluation/coercion, builtin return-type/edge-case behavior, autocommand/event ordering, and error/exception propagation.
    Diagnostics/test mode: emit warnings only within legacy scopes and provide a mode that records compatibility violations.
    Simple opt-ins: Lua decorators/attributes and a lightweight Vimscript modeline/local setting to declare required mode.
    Migration tooling: a command to run files/plugins under modern mode and report compatibility failures.

This preserves backwards compatibility for existing plugins while allowing Neovim to adopt safer, modern defaults for new code.

No thank you. That would be a massive effort for little gain -- Neovim and Vim have diverged enough for their "modern" versions to be essentially different editors. Their "legacy" version is still similar enough that you can share a simple(!) `.vimrc` and have it behave similarly, and nearly all "legacy" (vimscript) plugins work under Vim and Neovim. But there is zero interest in making Neovim "pretend" to be Vim; it also wouldn't solve any problem, just shift it to the point where you'd disable the "compatibility layer".

If there's an actual incompatibility that is a problem, open a focused issue for that.
We do provide the experimental `vim._with()` feature which approximates a kind of "transaction" context.

title: Proposal: Scoped compatibility modes (legacy ↔ modern) with per-command/context API

type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality
type.name: Enhancement
